summary: Jeff Atwood explains his decision to install a second video card for better Battlefield 3 performance, despite acknowledging it's overkill for most users. He details the technical requirements including motherboard compatibility, power supply needs, and cooling considerations. After troubleshooting issues with clock speeds and driver installations, he achieved nearly double the graphics performance. He frames this as a temporary solution until next-generation single GPUs arrive, which should match dual-card performance in a simpler package.
key_points: Multi-GPU setups using Alternate Frame Rendering can achieve roughly 1.8x performance, with diminishing returns beyond two cards
The Bitcoin mining crash created a glut of used video cards on eBay, making dual-GPU setups more affordable
Technical requirements include matching clock speeds exactly between cards, sufficient power supply (600W minimum), and careful attention to bus timing
Each new video card generation typically matches the performance of two previous-generation cards working together
Battlefield 3 was designed as a truly next-generation PC game that would stress even high-end systems of its era
money_quotes: But sometimes you gotta say to hell with rationality and embrace the overkill.
Since PC was the lead platform for Battlefield 3, it is the rare current game that isn't dumbed down to PS3 and Xbox 360 console levels.
The good news is that the market crash in BitCoin GPU mining (if you don't know what this is, don't ask… please) means there is a glut of recent video cards up for sale on eBay right now.
With every new video card introduction, you're supposed to get about the same performance in the new card as you did with two previous generation video cards working together.
A single fast video card is always going to be the simpler, easier, and cheaper route.
But after a (lot) of teething pains, I'm happy to report that multiple GPUs really does work as advertised.
key_insight: Multi-GPU setups offer a way to cheat time and preview next-generation performance, but the complexity and overhead mean a single powerful card remains the better choice for most users.
